skill.name: advanced-evaluation
skill.description: Guides teams in building LLM-as-judge evaluations, model comparisons, scoring rubrics, bias mitigation, and quality metrics.
skill.summary: Build reliable LLM output evaluations with rubrics, judge prompts, bias checks, and metrics.
skill.author: muratcankoylan

security_audit.summary: Static analysis produced many high and critical alerts, but review found they are false positives from Markdown code fences, JSON examples, and substring matches such as DES inside description. No prompt injection, credential access, shell execution, data exfiltration, or malicious intent was found; the only retained risk factor is an illustrative Python example script.

low_findings.title: Static Command-Execution Alerts Are Markdown Examples
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The external command findings point to fenced Markdown, prompt templates, and Python pseudocode examples. I found no shell execution APIs, subprocess use, or user-controlled command construction in the reviewed files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged locations are fenced examples or Markdown formulas, not executable shell syntax. The real Python script does not import subprocess or call shell execution functions.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are Substring Matches
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The weak cryptography alerts appear to match text fragments such as description, described, and descriptive. I found no MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or cryptographic implementation being used by the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact flagged lines contain evaluation metadata and rubric descriptions. Semantic review found no cryptographic API usage or security-sensitive hashing behavior.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Research Citations
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The hardcoded URLs are external research references in documentation. They are not fetched by code and do not transmit user data or credentials.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs appear under an External research heading and are not part of executable code. No HTTP client usage or network call was found in the script.

low_findings.title: Illustrative Python Script Requires Normal Review
low_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The package includes a runnable Python example script. The script demonstrates scoring, pairwise comparison, and rubric generation, but it does not access files, environment variables, credentials, networks, or external commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The script has a main block and can run locally, so the scripts risk factor is real. Its behavior is limited to in-memory examples and console output.

content.value_statement: Manual LLM evaluation is slow and inconsistent. This skill provides judge prompts, rubrics, bias checks, and metrics for repeatable output assessment.
actual_capabilities: Design direct scoring prompts with weighted criteria and evidence requirements.
Compare two model outputs with position swapping to reduce position bias.
Create domain-specific rubrics with score levels, examples, and edge cases.
Select metrics for classification, ordinal scoring, pairwise preference, and monitoring.
Identify length, verbosity, authority, and self-enhancement bias in evaluations.
Use the included Python example to understand evaluation flow and result aggregation.
limitations: It provides evaluation patterns and examples, not a hosted scoring service.
Actual scoring requires a separate model provider or evaluation runtime.
Automated judges still need validation against human review for important decisions.
Bias mitigation reduces known failure modes but cannot remove all evaluator bias.

use_cases.title: Compare candidate model outputs
use_cases.description: Run pairwise comparisons with position checks before selecting a prompt, model, or response strategy.

use_cases.title: Standardize response quality reviews
use_cases.description: Create shared rubrics so human and automated reviewers judge accuracy, clarity, and completeness consistently.

use_cases.title: Validate evaluator reliability
use_cases.description: Choose agreement, correlation, and bias metrics to compare automated judgments against human labels.

prompt_templates.title: Score one response
prompt_templates.prompt: Evaluate this response for accuracy, clarity, and completeness on a 1-5 scale. Use evidence from the response before giving each score. Prompt: {prompt}. Response: {response}.

prompt_templates.title: Compare two responses
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare Response A and Response B for the same prompt. Ignore response order and length unless length affects quality. Prompt: {prompt}. Response A: {response_a}. Response B: {response_b}.

prompt_templates.title: Create a rubric
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a 1-5 evaluation rubric for {criterion} in {domain}. Include level descriptions, observable characteristics, edge cases, and scoring guidance.

prompt_templates.title: Audit evaluator bias
prompt_templates.prompt: Review these evaluation results for position bias, length bias, verbosity bias, authority bias, and self-enhancement bias. Explain which metrics or reruns should validate the finding: {evaluation_results}.

output_examples.input: Evaluate a beginner explanation of quantum entanglement for accuracy, clarity, and completeness.
output: Accuracy: Strong, but the analogy simplifies important quantum details.
Clarity: Very strong for a general audience.
Completeness: Moderate because it omits measurement context and limitations.
Recommendation: Keep the analogy and add one sentence about quantum systems.

output_examples.input: Compare two answers explaining machine learning, one technical and one analogy-based.
output: Winner: The analogy-based response for a beginner audience.
Reason: It explains the concept with familiar examples and less jargon.
Bias check: Repeat with swapped positions before treating the result as final.

output_examples.input: Choose metrics for validating automated ratings against human reviewers.
output: Use Spearman correlation for ordinal scores.
Use Cohen kappa for categorical pass or fail judgments.
Track position consistency and length correlation as bias indicators.

best_practices: Require evidence and reasoning before each score to improve consistency.
Swap response positions in pairwise comparisons and lower confidence when results disagree.
Validate automated evaluations against human judgments before using them for important decisions.
anti_patterns: Using a single vague overall score without a rubric or evidence.
Treating one pairwise comparison as final without checking position bias.
Using the same model family to generate and judge outputs without noting self-enhancement risk.

faq.question: Does this skill run evaluations automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides prompts, rubrics, metric guidance, and an example script for building an evaluation workflow.

faq.question: Can it compare outputs from Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. It can guide comparisons for outputs from these tools when you provide the prompt, responses, and criteria.

faq.question: Which evaluation method should I start with?
faq.answer: Use direct scoring for objective criteria. Use pairwise comparison for subjective quality or preference decisions.

faq.question: How does it reduce position bias?
faq.answer: It recommends evaluating both response orders and mapping the second result back before deciding a winner.

faq.question: Does it remove the need for human reviewers?
faq.answer: No. Human review is still needed for calibration, edge cases, and high-stakes decisions.

faq.question: Is any data sent to external services by the skill itself?
faq.answer: No evidence found. The files contain documentation and examples, not network calls or credential access.
